description: SSH provides remote command-line access to a system. In labs and CTFs, SSH is often useful after finding valid usernames, passwords, private keys, or reused credentials from another service.
why_prioritize: SSH usually requires valid credentials, so it becomes more important after finding usernames, passwords, or private keys elsewhere.
The SSH banner, authentication methods, and host keys can reveal useful system details.
SSH access can confirm credentials and give an interactive foothold in authorized labs.

enumeration_steps.title: Check the SSH banner and version
enumeration_steps.reason: The banner can reveal the SSH server product, version, operating system hints, or custom messages.

enumeration_steps.title: Check SSH host keys and algorithms
enumeration_steps.reason: Host key and algorithm information can reveal weak configuration or repeated host keys across systems.

enumeration_steps.title: Try discovered credentials carefully
enumeration_steps.reason: SSH is commonly used to verify credentials found from FTP, SMB, web apps, databases, or config files.

enumeration_steps.title: Check authentication methods when you have a username
enumeration_steps.reason: Knowing which authentication methods are allowed can guide your next credential checks.

next_steps: Check whether the username appears in web pages, SMB shares, FTP files, email addresses, or source code.
Use the username when checking SSH authentication methods.
Look for password reuse across other services in the lab.
Keep a clear note of where the username was found.
Try the credential against SSH only in the authorized lab scope.
Check whether the same credential works for web logins, SMB, FTP, or databases.
After login, run basic host and user enumeration instead of jumping randomly.
Record which service accepted the credential.
Check file permissions and store the key safely in your lab notes folder.
Identify the likely username from filenames, comments, web content, or config files.
Try the key with ssh -i only against systems in scope.
If the key is encrypted, note that it may need a passphrase found elsewhere.
Check which commands are allowed.
Look for common shell escape paths through editors, pagers, or allowed binaries.
Check environment variables and PATH behavior.
Document the restriction before attempting bypasses in the lab.
Search for known issues affecting that exact product and version.
Confirm the version with Nmap and the banner before assuming it is accurate.
Treat the version as a lead, not proof of vulnerability.
Continue credential-focused enumeration because SSH usually needs authentication.

beginner_notes: SSH usually becomes useful after you find credentials somewhere else.
Do not brute force blindly. In labs, use gathered usernames and passwords with clear reasoning.
If a private key fails, check permissions, username, passphrase, and whether the key belongs to another host.
After SSH login, take notes before running many commands so you do not lose track.
